name: PowerShell Obfuscation and Execution Policy Bypass
description: Adversaries leverage PowerShell's rich scripting capabilities and frequent execution policy bypasses (-ExecutionPolicy Bypass, -ep bypass, -enc for base64-encoded payloads) to download and run malicious code. L2 analysts should be able to decode base64-encoded PowerShell commands (base64 -d or [System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode.GetString), identify AMSI bypass patterns, and correlate Script Block Logging (event 4104) with Module Logging (4103) to reconstruct obfuscated payloads. Tools: Revoke-Obfuscation, PSDecode.

name: Pass-the-Hash (Use Alternate Authentication Material)
description: Adversaries use captured NTLM hashes to authenticate without knowing the plaintext password. Detection relies on correlating event 4648 (explicit logon) clusters from a single source with anomalous logon types: Logon Type 3 (network) using NTLM authentication (4776) when Kerberos would be expected, and Logon Type 9 (NewCredentials). Mimikatz sekurlsa::pth leaves a characteristic footprint: a new logon session created under an existing process. Mitigation: enforce Kerberos-only (disable NTLMv1/v2), deploy Windows Defender Credential Guard.

name: Kerberoasting (Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets)
description: Adversaries request Kerberos service tickets (TGS) for service accounts, then extract and offline-crack the ticket's RC4 or AES encrypted blob to recover the service account password. Detection: event 4769 (TGS requested) with Ticket Encryption Type 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) from a non-service account is the primary signal. Tools used: Rubeus, Invoke-Kerberoast. Mitigation: enforce AES encryption only (0x12/0x11), use Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) with 120-char random passwords that cannot be cracked.

name: Ransomware — Data Encrypted for Impact
description: Ransomware encrypts victim files to cause service disruption and demand payment. L2 analysts lead ransomware response: immediate network segmentation of affected hosts, shadow-copy deletion forensics (event 524 or VSS API calls via T1490), identification of the initial access vector (phishing T1566 or exploitation T1190), and ransom note artifact collection for variant attribution. The first 15 minutes decision window: contain vs. let run for intelligence — organizational IR policy drives this. Recovery from backup is preferred over payment.

name: C2 Application Layer Protocol (Beacon Detection)
description: Adversaries encode C2 traffic inside standard protocols (HTTP/S, DNS, SMTP) to avoid firewall blocking. Cobalt Strike beacons produce characteristic fixed-interval HTTP GET/POST (default jitter 0%, check-in every ~60s). Detection: compute inter-request timing variance for each internal host; low-jitter, low-variance connections to rare external IPs are beacon candidates. JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprints and HASSH SSH fingerprints enable detection even when content is encrypted. Tools: RITA (Real Intelligence Threat Analytics), Zeek network logs.

name: Lateral Movement Tactic Detection
description: Lateral movement involves adversaries moving through the network to reach high-value targets after initial compromise. L2 analysts correlate multiple sub-techniques: PsExec (T1570 — remote service creation + ADMIN$ share access), WMI (T1047 — WmiPrvSE.exe spawning child processes), WinRM (T1021.006 — port 5985/5986 logon), and RDP (T1021.001 — event 4624 Logon Type 10 from unusual source). Beaconing from the compromised host to multiple internal peers within minutes of initial compromise is a key tipping point for lateral movement escalation.

name: Active Directory Account and Group Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate accounts, groups, and permissions to identify high-privilege targets. T1087.002 (Domain Account) uses net user /domain, Get-ADUser, and ldapsearch to list all AD accounts. Detection: high-volume LDAP queries (event 1644 in DC trace logs), net.exe execution with /domain flag (event 4688), and BloodHound/SharpHound collection patterns (compressed LDAP queries bursting from a single host over <5 minutes). LDAP signing and channel binding enforcement reduces the attack surface.

name: Impair Defenses (Defense Evasion)
description: Adversaries disable or tamper with security tools to reduce detection likelihood. Sub-techniques include: T1562.001 (disabling AV/EDR via registry or sc.exe stop), T1562.002 (disabling Windows Event Logging via auditpol.exe or NtTraceControl), T1562.004 (modifying firewall rules via netsh advfirewall). Detection: event 4719 (system audit policy changed), 7036 (service stopped) and 7040 (service start type changed) for EDR services, and Sysmon event 12/13 for registry modifications to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<EDR>.
